title: Corneal Abrasion
oneLiner: Acute defect in corneal epithelium causing severe pain, photophobia – common in young / trauma / contact lens wear; risk of infection if mismanaged.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Corneal ulcer / infectious keratitis** (especially contact lens wearer) – single finding: **hypopyon** (pus in anterior chamber) or white corneal infiltrate; can perforate → endophthalmitis in hours.
- **Penetrating globe injury** – single finding: **Seidel sign** (fluorescein streaming from wound) or shallow / flat anterior chamber.
- **Acute angle closure glaucoma** – single finding: **rock-hard eye** on palpation + mid-dilated fixed pupil + markedly raised IOP; not relieved by topical anaesthesia.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Intense ocular pain (worse with blinking / light)
  - Photophobia, tearing, foreign body sensation
  - Blurred vision (if abrasion over visual axis)
- Key exam findings:
  - Reduced visual acuity (measure each eye)
  - Conjunctival injection, possible chemosis
  - Positive fluorescein stain (green defect under cobalt blue light)
  - Corneal sensation normal (if reduced → herpetic)
  - Check for foreign body (evert upper lid, look in fornices)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely → less likely:
  - Simple traumatic abrasion (clear history, no infiltrate)
  - Recurrent erosion syndrome (pain on awakening, prior abrasion)
  - Corneal foreign body (seen on slit lamp, often subtarsal)
  - Corneal ulcer / microbial keratitis (infiltrate, discharge, hypopyon)
  - Herpes simplex keratitis (dendritic ulcer with fluorescein, reduced sensation)
  - Uveitis (circumcorneal injection, cells + flare in anterior chamber)
  - Acute angle closure glaucoma (elevated IOP, fixed mid-dilated pupil)
- **Splitter:** Fluorescein pattern – linear / geographic defect → simple abrasion; branching dendritic pattern → herpes simplex; focal white infiltrate → microbial keratitis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Fluorescein staining (single-use vial) + slit lamp (or blue light)
  - Seidel test (fluorescein stream under high magnification)
  - Measure visual acuity
  - If contact lens wearer – remove lens immediately and take for gram stain / culture (refer)
- **Bloods:** Not routine – only if suspect sepsis / severe infection (FBC, CRP, blood cultures)
- **Imaging:** Not routine – consider orbital XR / CT if suspected intraocular foreign body (history of metal on metal, grinding)
- **Special tests:** Corneal scraping / culture if ulcer present (send for gram stain, culture and sensitivity)
- **Never-miss test:** **Fluorescein staining** – omission = missed diagnosis of abrasion / dendrite; also check for contact lens wear (ask specifically).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Remove contact lens (if present) – crucial
  - Topical antibiotic drops:
    - *No contact lens*: chloramphenicol 0.5% drops 1-2 drops QDS for 5-7 days
    - *Contact lens wearer*: ofloxacin 0.3% drops 1-2 drops every 2 hours (waking) for 24 hours then QDS; refer to ophthalmology
  - Cycloplegic (pressure-pain relief):
    - Cyclopentolate 1% 1 drop TDS (or homatropine 1% TDS)
  - Oral analgesia: paracetamol 1g QDS ± ibuprofen 400mg TDS
  - **Do NOT patch** – no longer recommended (may promote infection, especially contact lens)
  - **Do NOT use topical anaesthetic** for discharge – masks worsening, delays diagnosis, causes epithelial toxicity
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Only admit if:
    - Contact lens-related infection / large central ulcer
    - Systemically unwell / immunocompromised
    - Unable to tolerate drops
    - Suspected perforation or endophthalmitis
  - If admitted: intensive topical antibiotics (e.g. ofloxacin q1-2h) + cycloplegic + analgesia
  - Daily slit lamp review (ophthalmology)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lubricating drops (e.g. hypromellose) if recurrent erosions
  - Topical lubricating ointment at bedtime for recurrent erosion syndrome
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** nil specific – maintain hydration
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not needed unless immobile / other risk factors
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 chart; eye observations (visual acuity, pain score) at each assessment
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops: 1-2 drops QDS for 5-7 days (or ointment at bedtime)
  - Alternative (if contact lens / suspected gram-negative): ofloxacin 0.3% drops 1-2 drops q2h for 24h then QDS – **[check local policy for exact frequency]**
  - Cycloplegic: cyclopentolate 1% 1 drop TDS
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Fusidic acid 1% gel QDS (if chloramphenicol allergic) – not for contact lens
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Topical steroids** – absolute contraindication in active abrasion/ulcer (risk of fungal / viral progression)
  - Chloramphenicol – avoid if history of bone marrow suppression (rare) – but first-line for non-contact lens
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** nil specific – topical drops minimal systemic absorption
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prescribing topical steroids** for suspected abrasion (hides infection, worsens ulcer)
  - **Patching a contact lens wearer** – creates warm, moist environment for Pseudomonas to flourish
  - **Giving topical anaesthetic for take-home use** – allows corneal damage to progress unrecognised
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Corneal ulcer / microbial keratitis (especially Pseudomonas in contact lens wear)
  - Recurrent erosion syndrome (persistent epithelial defect, pain on awakening)
  - Hypopyon – indicates severe infection
  - Secondary iritis (if large abrasion)
- **Chronic:**
  - Corneal scarring (visual loss)
  - Post-herpetic neuralgia (if herpes simplex)
  - Endophthalmitis (rare – from neglected infection)
sections.Prognosis: - Most simple abrasions heal in 24-72h without scarring – excellent prognosis. Larger / central abrasions may take longer. Recurrent erosion occurs in ~10-30% (especially after trauma with shearing force). Contact lens-related infections can progress rapidly to visual loss if not treated promptly.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always evert the upper lid** to look for subtarsal foreign body – most common missed cause of persistent pain.
- **Use single-use fluorescein vials** – multi-use bottles can grow Pseudomonas.
- **Document contact lens wear status** in the notes – changes antibiotic choice and referral urgency.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not patch** – especially in contact lens wearers (increases infection risk).
- **Do not use topical anaesthetic** for more than immediate examination – delays proper treatment, causes toxicity.
- **Do not prescribe topical steroids** – can convert a simple abrasion into a fungal/HSV ulcer.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** any worsening of pain, photophobia, redness, discharge, blurred vision, or if not improved in 48 hours.
- **Return immediately if:** contact lens wearer develops new pain, hypopyon, or sudden visual loss.
- **Follow-up:** GP or optician in 48 hours for simple abrasion; same-day ophthalmology review if contact lens-related.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ophthalmology (same-day):**
  - Suspected corneal ulcer with hypopyon or infiltrate
  - Contact lens-related abrasion + any signs of infection
  - Seidel positive (perforation)
  - Worsening pain despite analgesia
- **Call crash team / ICU:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (tachycardia, fever) in contact lens wearer
  - Sepsis with eye signs – suspect endophthalmitis / orbital cellulitis
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L + systemic symptoms
  - GCS <15 or signs of meningism (rare – but consider if severe infection)

rawMarkdown:
## Corneal Abrasion
**One-liner:** Acute defect in corneal epithelium causing severe pain, photophobia – common in young / trauma / contact lens wear; risk of infection if mismanaged.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Corneal ulcer / infectious keratitis** (especially contact lens wearer) – single finding: **hypopyon** (pus in anterior chamber) or white corneal infiltrate; can perforate → endophthalmitis in hours.
- **Penetrating globe injury** – single finding: **Seidel sign** (fluorescein streaming from wound) or shallow / flat anterior chamber.
- **Acute angle closure glaucoma** – single finding: **rock-hard eye** on palpation + mid-dilated fixed pupil + markedly raised IOP; not relieved by topical anaesthesia.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Intense ocular pain (worse with blinking / light)
  - Photophobia, tearing, foreign body sensation
  - Blurred vision (if abrasion over visual axis)
- Key exam findings:
  - Reduced visual acuity (measure each eye)
  - Conjunctival injection, possible chemosis
  - Positive fluorescein stain (green defect under cobalt blue light)
  - Corneal sensation normal (if reduced → herpetic)
  - Check for foreign body (evert upper lid, look in fornices)

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely → less likely:
  - Simple traumatic abrasion (clear history, no infiltrate)
  - Recurrent erosion syndrome (pain on awakening, prior abrasion)
  - Corneal foreign body (seen on slit lamp, often subtarsal)
  - Corneal ulcer / microbial keratitis (infiltrate, discharge, hypopyon)
  - Herpes simplex keratitis (dendritic ulcer with fluorescein, reduced sensation)
  - Uveitis (circumcorneal injection, cells + flare in anterior chamber)
  - Acute angle closure glaucoma (elevated IOP, fixed mid-dilated pupil)
- **Splitter:** Fluorescein pattern – linear / geographic defect → simple abrasion; branching dendritic pattern → herpes simplex; focal white infiltrate → microbial keratitis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Fluorescein staining (single-use vial) + slit lamp (or blue light)
  - Seidel test (fluorescein stream under high magnification)
  - Measure visual acuity
  - If contact lens wearer – remove lens immediately and take for gram stain / culture (refer)
- **Bloods:** Not routine – only if suspect sepsis / severe infection (FBC, CRP, blood cultures)
- **Imaging:** Not routine – consider orbital XR / CT if suspected intraocular foreign body (history of metal on metal, grinding)
- **Special tests:** Corneal scraping / culture if ulcer present (send for gram stain, culture and sensitivity)
- **Never-miss test:** **Fluorescein staining** – omission = missed diagnosis of abrasion / dendrite; also check for contact lens wear (ask specifically).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Remove contact lens (if present) – crucial
  - Topical antibiotic drops:
    - *No contact lens*: chloramphenicol 0.5% drops 1-2 drops QDS for 5-7 days
    - *Contact lens wearer*: ofloxacin 0.3% drops 1-2 drops every 2 hours (waking) for 24 hours then QDS; refer to ophthalmology
  - Cycloplegic (pressure-pain relief):
    - Cyclopentolate 1% 1 drop TDS (or homatropine 1% TDS)
  - Oral analgesia: paracetamol 1g QDS ± ibuprofen 400mg TDS
  - **Do NOT patch** – no longer recommended (may promote infection, especially contact lens)
  - **Do NOT use topical anaesthetic** for discharge – masks worsening, delays diagnosis, causes epithelial toxicity
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Only admit if:
    - Contact lens-related infection / large central ulcer
    - Systemically unwell / immunocompromised
    - Unable to tolerate drops
    - Suspected perforation or endophthalmitis
  - If admitted: intensive topical antibiotics (e.g. ofloxacin q1-2h) + cycloplegic + analgesia
  - Daily slit lamp review (ophthalmology)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lubricating drops (e.g. hypromellose) if recurrent erosions
  - Topical lubricating ointment at bedtime for recurrent erosion syndrome
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** nil specific – maintain hydration
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not needed unless immobile / other risk factors
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 chart; eye observations (visual acuity, pain score) at each assessment

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops: 1-2 drops QDS for 5-7 days (or ointment at bedtime)
  - Alternative (if contact lens / suspected gram-negative): ofloxacin 0.3% drops 1-2 drops q2h for 24h then QDS – **[check local policy for exact frequency]**
  - Cycloplegic: cyclopentolate 1% 1 drop TDS
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Fusidic acid 1% gel QDS (if chloramphenicol allergic) – not for contact lens
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Topical steroids** – absolute contraindication in active abrasion/ulcer (risk of fungal / viral progression)
  - Chloramphenicol – avoid if history of bone marrow suppression (rare) – but first-line for non-contact lens
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** nil specific – topical drops minimal systemic absorption
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prescribing topical steroids** for suspected abrasion (hides infection, worsens ulcer)
  - **Patching a contact lens wearer** – creates warm, moist environment for Pseudomonas to flourish
  - **Giving topical anaesthetic for take-home use** – allows corneal damage to progress unrecognised

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Corneal ulcer / microbial keratitis (especially Pseudomonas in contact lens wear)
  - Recurrent erosion syndrome (persistent epithelial defect, pain on awakening)
  - Hypopyon – indicates severe infection
  - Secondary iritis (if large abrasion)
- **Chronic:**
  - Corneal scarring (visual loss)
  - Post-herpetic neuralgia (if herpes simplex)
  - Endophthalmitis (rare – from neglected infection)

### Prognosis
- Most simple abrasions heal in 24-72h without scarring – excellent prognosis. Larger / central abrasions may take longer. Recurrent erosion occurs in ~10-30% (especially after trauma with shearing force). Contact lens-related infections can progress rapidly to visual loss if not treated promptly.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always evert the upper lid** to look for subtarsal foreign body – most common missed cause of persistent pain.
- **Use single-use fluorescein vials** – multi-use bottles can grow Pseudomonas.
- **Document contact lens wear status** in the notes – changes antibiotic choice and referral urgency.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not patch** – especially in contact lens wearers (increases infection risk).
- **Do not use topical anaesthetic** for more than immediate examination – delays proper treatment, causes toxicity.
- **Do not prescribe topical steroids** – can convert a simple abrasion into a fungal/HSV ulcer.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** any worsening of pain, photophobia, redness, discharge, blurred vision, or if not improved in 48 hours.
- **Return immediately if:** contact lens wearer develops new pain, hypopyon, or sudden visual loss.
- **Follow-up:** GP or optician in 48 hours for simple abrasion; same-day ophthalmology review if contact lens-related.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ophthalmology (same-day):**
  - Suspected corneal ulcer with hypopyon or infiltrate
  - Contact lens-related abrasion + any signs of infection
  - Seidel positive (perforation)
  - Worsening pain despite analgesia
- **Call crash team / ICU:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (tachycardia, fever) in contact lens wearer
  - Sepsis with eye signs – suspect endophthalmitis / orbital cellulitis
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L + systemic symptoms
  - GCS <15 or signs of meningism (rare – but consider if severe infection)
